# The Star Weaver's Gift

Long ago, before dawn existed, the world lay trapped in endless night. The people huddled around small fires, afraid of the darkness that pressed against their camps.

In the highest mountain lived an old woman who spent her nights weaving. Her fingers moved ceaselessly, pulling silver threads from moonbeams and starlight. The people below never saw her, but sometimes they noticed new stars appearing in the sky.

One winter, the cold grew so bitter that even the stars seemed to freeze. The people's fires began to die. A brave child climbed the mountain to beg for help.

The Star Weaver listened to the child's plea. She realized her beautiful tapestries meant nothing if the people perished. With trembling hands, she unraveled her greatest work—a magnificent cloth containing a thousand stars.

"Take this," she said, handing the child a brilliant golden thread she'd saved for centuries. "Plant it in the eastern hills."

The child obeyed. From that thread grew the first sunrise, bringing warmth and hope to the world.

The Star Weaver still sits on her mountain, weaving each sunset and sunrise. And if you watch carefully at dawn, you can see silver threads connecting the fading stars—remnants of her endless, loving labor.

Aethon's heart ached with longing. "What joy is life without expression?" the spirit cried out. This cry shattered the eternal silence, creating the first sound—a pure note that echoed across all creation.

The birds heard this wondrous revelation and opened their beaks. From their throats poured melodies, each species discovering its own unique voice. The wind, inspired by their songs, began to hum through canyons and whistle through trees. Rivers discovered rhythm in their flow over stones. Even the stones themselves learned to sing when struck together.

Humans, the last creation, received the greatest gift: the ability to form words into songs, to craft instruments from wood and string, and to weave all of nature's sounds into harmony.

Thus music was born from loneliness and tears, becoming the universal language that connects all living things, reminding us we are never truly alone in this singing world.
